{{Year nav topic5|2012|science}}
{{Science year nav|2012}}
{{Use dmy dates|date=October 2023}}
{{Main|2012 in science}}
This article lists a number of significant events in [[science]] that have occurred in the first quarter of [[2012]].

==Events==
===January===
[[File:GRAIL.jpg|thumb|right|250px|1 January 2012: [[NASA]]'s twin [[Gravity Recovery and Interior Laboratory|GRAIL]] satellites (artist's impression shown) begin studying the Moon's gravitational field.]]
* 1 January – [[NASA]]'s [[Gravity Recovery and Interior Laboratory|GRAIL-B]] satellite successfully enters [[Moon|lunar]] orbit, joining its twin spacecraft GRAIL-A. The two satellites will study the Moon's gravitational field, generating a detailed map of its fluctuations to help scientists understand how the Moon formed.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.nasa.gov/mission_pages/grail/news/grail20120101.html |title=NASA's Twin Grail Spacecraft Reunite in Lunar Orbit |publisher=BBC |date=1 January 2012 |access-date=12 February 2013 |archive-date=2 January 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120102200832/http://www.nasa.gov/mission_pages/grail/news/grail20120101.html }}</ref>
* 2 January
** China launches its first commercial [[3DTV]] channel, operated by [[China Central Television]] (CCTV).<ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/world-asia-china-16381069 |title=China launches first 3D TV channel |date=2 January 2013 |access-date=12 February 2013 |publisher=BBC News}}</ref>
** A new study shows that [[deep brain stimulation]] (DBS) is a safe and effective intervention for [[treatment-resistant depression]] in patients with either [[Unipolar depression|unipolar major depressive disorder]] (MDD) or [[Bipolar 2 disorder|bipolar ll disorder]] (BP).<ref>{{cite web |url=http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-01-deep-brain-results-unipolar-bipolar.html |publisher=MedicalXpress |title=Deep brain stimulation shows promising results for unipolar and bipolar depression |date=2 February 2012 |access-date=12 February 2013}}</ref>
* 3 January – [[Genetic modification|Genetically modified]] fast-ageing [[mice]] exhibited improved health and lived two to three times longer than expected after being injected with [[stem cells]], according to findings published in ''Nature Communications''.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://news.nationalgeographic.com/news/2012/01/120106-aging-mice-stem-cells-old-young-science-health |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120109030253/http://news.nationalgeographic.com/news/2012/01/120106-aging-mice-stem-cells-old-young-science-health/ |archive-date=9 January 2012 |work=National Geographic |title=Old Mice Made "Young"—May Lead to Anti-Aging Treatments |date=6 January 2012 |access-date=12 February 2013}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |vauthors=Lavasani M, Robinson AR, Lu A, Song M, Feduska JM, Ahani B, Tilstra JS, Feldman CH, Robbins PD, Niedernhofer LJ, Huard J |title=Muscle-derived stem/progenitor cell dysfunction limits healthspan and lifespan in a murine progeria model |journal=Nature Communications |volume=3 |article-number=608 |date=January 2012 |pmid=22215083 |pmc=3272577 |doi=10.1038/ncomms1611 |bibcode=2012NatCo...3..608L}}</ref>
* 4 January
** American scientists report that a [[parasite|parasitic]] species of fly which compels [[honey bee]]s to abandon their hives may be responsible for a global [[Colony collapse disorder|honey bee die-off]] that has decimated hives around the world. Honey bees are crucial [[pollinator]]s, and their rapidly diminishing population may have severe effects on human [[agriculture]].<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.huffingtonpost.com/2012/01/04/bee-die-off-parasitic-fly_n_1183947.html |agency=Associated Press |work= Huffington Post |title=Bee Die-Off: Parasitic Fly Could Explain Phenomenon |date=4 January 2012 |access-date=12 February 2013}}</ref>
** [[University of Wyoming]] scientists develop genetically modified [[silkworm]]s capable of producing large amounts of [[spidersilk]], which has a greater [[tensile strength]] than [[steel]]. If available in bulk quantities, the silk could be used to produce high-strength medical [[Surgical suture|sutures]] and lightweight forms of [[body armor]].<ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/science-environment-16399257|title=GM silk worms make Spider-Man web closer to reality |date=4 January 2012 |access-date=12 February 2013 |publisher=BBC News |last1=Ghosh |first1=Pallab}}</ref>
** Scientists at the [[University of Southern California]] develop a method for generating accurate 3D models of cellular [[genome]]s.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.physorg.com/news/2012-01-scientists-d-human-genome.html |title=Scientists create first 3-D map of human genome |publisher=PhysOrg |date=4 January 2012 |access-date=12 February 2013}}</ref>
** Researchers at [[Oxford University]] report promising results in human trials of a prototype [[hepatitis C]] vaccine.<ref>{{Cite journal |title=Novel Adenovirus-Based Vaccines Induce Broad and Sustained T Cell Responses to HCV in Man |volume=4 |issue=115 |pages=115ra1 |journal=Science Translational Medicine |date=4 January 2012 |doi=10.1126/scitranslmed.3003155 |pmid=22218690 |pmc=3627207 |last1=Barnes |first1=E. |last2=Folgori |first2=A. |last3=Capone |first3=S. |display-authors=1}}</ref>
** Scientists at [[Cornell University]] use a specialised lens to entirely cloak an object from view for 40 trillionths of a second by altering the [[speed of light]].<ref>{{cite journal |author4-link=Alexander Gaeta |vauthors=Fridman M, Farsi A, Okawachi Y, Gaeta AL |title=Demonstration of temporal cloaking |journal=Nature |volume=481 |issue=7379 |pages=62–5 |date=January 2012 |pmid=22222748 |doi=10.1038/nature10695 |bibcode=2012Natur.481...62F |arxiv=1107.2062 |s2cid=4421175 }}</ref>
** Classified documents are leaked detailing a range of advanced [[non-lethal weapons]] proposed or in development by the [[United States Armed Forces]]. Among the systems described are a [[laser]]-based weapon designed to divert hostile aircraft, an underwater [[sonic weapon]] for incapacitating [[SCUBA diver]]s and a heat-based weapon designed to compel crowds to disperse.<ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/technology-16415007 |title=US non-lethal weapon 'wish list' revealed on the net |date=4 January 2012 |access-date=16 February 2013 |publisher=BBC News}}</ref>
[[File:Silkworms3000px.jpg|thumb|right|250px|4 January 2012: scientists create [[genetic engineering|genetically engineered]] [[silkworms]] capable of producing bulk quantities of steel-strong [[spidersilk]].]]
* 5 January
** [[Mae Jemison]], the first female [[African-American]] astronaut, is selected to head the [[DARPA]]- and [[NASA]]-sponsored 100-Year Starship project, which aims to conduct research into the technological and human elements needed for [[Interstellar travel#100 Year Starship study|crewed interstellar travel]].<ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/science-environment-16427876 |title=Former astronaut to lead starship effort|date=5 January 2012 |access-date=16 February 2013 |publisher=BBC News |last1=Weinberger |first1=Sharon}}</ref>
** American scientists report that they have bred the first-ever [[monkey]]s grown from cells taken from different [[embryo]]s.<ref>{{cite journal |vauthors=Tachibana M, Sparman M, Ramsey C, Ma H, Lee HS, Penedo MC, Mitalipov S |title=Generation of chimeric rhesus monkeys |journal=Cell |volume=148 |issue=1–2 |pages=285–95 |date=January 2012 |pmid=22225614 |pmc=3264685 |doi=10.1016/j.cell.2011.12.007 }}</ref>
** A team of international researchers reports that [[electrical resistance|low-resistivity]] electrical wires can be produced at the [[nanometer]] scale by chaining [[phosphorus]] atoms together and encasing them in [[silicon]]. In future, the development may permit the production of [[nanoelectronics|efficient nanometer-scale electronics]].<ref>{{cite web |title=Ohm Run: One-Atom-Tall Wires Could Extend Life of Moore's Law |url=http://www.scientificamerican.com/article.cfm?id=nanowires-silicon |work=Scientific American |date=5 January 2013 |access-date=16 February 2013}}</ref>
** A team of American, French and Italian researchers demonstrate working [[transistor]]s made from [[cotton]] fibers, doped with gold [[nanoparticle]]s and a conductive polymer. The invention could permit the creation of a range of electronic-fabric devices, including clothing capable of measuring pollutants, T-shirts that display dynamic information, and carpets that sense how many people are crossing them.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://spectrum.ieee.org/electronic-cotton |title=Electronic Cotton |publisher=IEEE Spectrum |date=January 2012 |access-date=16 February 2013}}</ref>
* 6 January
** The human brain's ability to function can start to deteriorate as early as [[Middle age|age 45]], according to a study published in the ''[[British Medical Journal]]''.<ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/health-16425522|title=Brain function can start declining 'as early as age 45'  |date=6 January 2012 |access-date=10 March 2013 |publisher=BBC News}}</ref>
** Scientists refute a [[Greenpeace]] claim that [[GM food|genetically modified corn]] has caused a new [[Pest (organism)#Insects|insect pest]].<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.ingentaconnect.com/content/esa/jipm/2011/00000002/00000003/art00003 |title=Genetically Engineered Bt Corn and Range Expansion of the Western Bean Cutworm (Lepidoptera: Noctuidae) in the United States: A Response to Greenpeace Germany |work=Journal of Integrated Pest Management |date=1 December 2011 |access-date=10 March 2013}}</ref>
[[File:UpsilonAndromedae D moons.jpg|thumb|right|250px|11 January 2012: astronomers report that nearly every star in the [[Milky Way galaxy]] may host [[exoplanet]]s (artist's impression of [[Upsilon Andromedae d]] pictured).]]
* 9 January
** Human emissions of [[carbon dioxide]] will defer the next [[ice age]], according to a new study.<ref>{{cite journal |title=Determining the natural length of the current interglacial |journal=Nature Geoscience |date=9 January 2012 |volume=5 |issue=2 |doi=10.1038/ngeo1358 |pages=138–141 |bibcode=2012NatGe...5..138T |last1=Tzedakis |first1=P. C. |last2=Channell |first2=J. E. T. |last3=Hodell |first3=D. A. |display-authors=1}}</ref>
** Researchers in California develop a cheap plastic capable of removing large amounts of carbon dioxide (CO<sub>2</sub>) from the air. The new material could enable the development of "artificial trees" that lower atmospheric concentrations of CO<sub>2</sub> in an effort to lessen the [[effects of climate change]].<ref>{{cite web |url=http://news.sciencemag.org/sciencenow/2012/01/new-co2-sucker-could-help-clear-.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120111160710/http://news.sciencemag.org/sciencenow/2012/01/new-co2-sucker-could-help-clear-.html |archive-date=11 January 2012 |title=New CO<sub>2</sub> Sucker Could Help Clear the Air |work=Science |date=9 January 2012 |access-date=17 April 2013}}</ref>
* 10 January
** The 2012 [[Consumer Electronics Show]] opens in [[Las Vegas Valley|Las Vegas]], [[Nevada]]. Among the new products and technologies showcased are large-screen [[OLED]] televisions, [[multi-core processor|quad-core]] [[tablet computer]]s and consumer-ready [[3D printer]]s.<ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/technology-16503443 |title='Affordable' 3D printers at CES |last=Kelion |first=Leo |name-list-style=vanc |date=2012-03-08 |publisher=BBC News |access-date=2018-05-04 |language=en-GB}}</ref>
** Climate change, in the form of reduced snowfall in mountains, is having a major impact on mountainous plant and bird communities, through the increased ability of [[elk]] to stay at high elevations over winter and consume plants, according to a study in ''Nature Climate Change''.<ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2012/01/120110140235.htm |title=Dramatic links found between climate change, elk, plants, and birds |date=2012-01-11 |work=ScienceDaily |access-date=2018-05-04 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Martin |first1=Thomas E. |last2=Maron |first2=John L. |name-list-style=vanc |date=2012-01-10 |title=Climate impacts on bird and plant communities from altered animal–plant interactions |journal=Nature Climate Change |volume=2 |issue=3 |pages=195–200 |doi=10.1038/nclimate1348 |issn=1758-678X |bibcode=2012NatCC...2..195M |url=https://zenodo.org/record/1233361}}</ref> 
* 11 January
** An international team of astronomers report that each star in the [[Milky Way Galaxy]] may host "[[exoplanet|on average ... at least 1.6 planets]]", suggesting that over 160 billion star-bound planets may exist in our galaxy alone. The team used [[gravitational lensing|gravitational microlensing]] to discover the gravitational effects of planets orbiting distant stars.<ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/science-environment-16515944 |title=Study says most stars have planet |last=Palmer |first=Jason |name-list-style=vanc |date=2012 |publisher=BBC News |access-date=2018-05-05 |language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |url=http://www.space.com/14200-160-billion-alien-planets-milky-galaxy.html |title=160 Billion Alien Planets May Exist in Our Milky Way Galaxy |work=Space.com |access-date=2018-05-05}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |vauthors=Cassan A, Kubas D, Beaulieu JP, Dominik M, Horne K, Greenhill J, Wambsganss J, Menzies J, Williams A, Jørgensen UG, Udalski A, Bennett DP, Albrow MD, Batista V, Brillant S, Caldwell JA, Cole A, Coutures C, Cook KH, Dieters S, Prester DD, Donatowicz J, Fouqué P, Hill K, Kains N, Kane S, Marquette JB, Martin R, Pollard KR, Sahu KC, Vinter C, Warren D, Watson B, Zub M, Sumi T, Szymański MK, Kubiak M, Poleski R, Soszynski I, Ulaczyk K, Pietrzyński G, Wyrzykowski L |display-authors=6 |title=One or more bound planets per Milky Way star from microlensing observations |language=En |journal=Nature |volume=481 |issue=7380 |pages=167–9 |date=January 2012 |pmid=22237108 |doi=10.1038/nature10684 |arxiv=1202.0903 |bibcode=2012Natur.481..167C |s2cid=2614136 }}</ref>
** American astronomers discover three rocky [[exoplanet]]s smaller than Earth, the smallest such worlds yet found, orbiting a [[red dwarf]] star 130 [[light-year]]s from Earth.<ref>{{Cite magazine |url=https://www.wired.com/wiredscience/2012/01/smallest-exoplanets/#more-92158 |title=3 Rocky Worlds are Smallest Exoplanets Found to Date |last=Mann |first=Adam |name-list-style=vanc |date=2012-01-11 |magazine=WIRED |access-date=2018-05-05 |language=en-US}}</ref>
** Researchers report the discovery of a natural [[hormone]] that has a similar effect to [[exercise]] on [[muscle]] tissue – burning [[calorie]]s, improving [[insulin]] processing, and perhaps boosting strength.<ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.technologyreview.com/biomedicine/39449/?p1=A1 |title=Discovery Could Lead to an Exercise Pill |last=Weintraub |first=Karen |name-list-style=vanc |work=MIT Technology Review |access-date=2018-05-05 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |vauthors=Boström P, Wu J, Jedrychowski MP, Korde A, Ye L, Lo JC, Rasbach KA, Boström EA, Choi JH, Long JZ, Kajimura S, Zingaretti MC, Vind BF, Tu H, Cinti S, Højlund K, Gygi SP, Spiegelman BM |title=A PGC1-α-dependent myokine that drives brown-fat-like development of white fat and thermogenesis |language=En |journal=Nature |volume=481 |issue=7382 |pages=463–8 |date=January 2012 |pmid=22237023 |pmc=3522098 |doi=10.1038/nature10777 |bibcode=2012Natur.481..463B }}</ref>
* 12 January
** Scientists formally describe the world's smallest known [[vertebrate]] species, ''[[Paedophryne amauensis]]'' – a [[frog]] that measures just 7 millimeters in length. The species was first discovered in [[Papua New Guinea]] in 2009.<ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.theguardian.com/environment/2012/jan/12/world-smallest-frog |title=Tiny frog claimed as world's smallest vertebrate |date=2012-01-12 |newspaper=The Guardian |language=en |access-date=2018-05-05 |agency=Associated Press }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |vauthors=Rittmeyer EN, Allison A, Gründler MC, Thompson DK, Austin CC |title=Ecological guild evolution and the discovery of the world's smallest vertebrate |journal=PLOS ONE |volume=7 |issue=1 |article-number=e29797 |date=2012-01-11 |pmid=22253785 |pmc=3256195 |doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0029797 |bibcode=2012PLoSO...729797R |doi-access=free }}</ref> 
** A [[University of Connecticut]] researcher who studied the health benefits of [[resveratrol]], a compound found in [[Health effects of wine|red wine]], has been found to have falsified data on numerous occasions.<ref>{{Cite news |url=http://www.medicalnewstoday.com/articles/240222.php |title=Researcher Who Studied Benefits of Red Wine Falsified Data Says University |last=Paddock |first=Catherine |name-list-style=vanc |date=2012-01-12 |work=Medical News Today |access-date=2018-05-05 |language=en}}</ref>

[[File:Paratype of Paedophryne amauensis (LSUMZ 95004).png|thumb|right|250px|12 January 2012: ''[[Paedophryne amauensis]]'', the world's smallest known [[vertebrate]], is formally described.]]
* 13 January
** [[IBM]] researchers successfully store a single [[bit]] of data in a group of just 12 supercooled [[iron]] atoms; current commercial [[hard disk]]s require over 1 million atoms to store one bit of data. The breakthrough, which was achieved with the use of a [[scanning tunnelling microscope]], may permit the production of ultra-high-density computer [[storage media]] in future.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/technology-16543497 |title=IBM researchers make 12-atom magnetic memory bit |date=2012-03-08 |access-date=2020-10-23 |publisher=BBC}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.ecommercetimes.com/story/74178.html |publisher=E-Commerce Times |author=Richard Adhikari |title=IBM Discovers How to Store Data in a Dozen Atoms |date=2012-01-13 |access-date=2020-10-23 |archive-date=26 November 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201126000628/https://www.ecommercetimes.com/story/74178.html |url-status=dead }}</ref>
** German scientists convert a gold sphere just 60 nanometres in diameter into an ultra-sensitive [[listening device]], potentially allowing the sounds of bacteria and other single-celled organisms to be recorded.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.newscientist.com/article/mg21328475.100-gold-nano-ears-set-to-listen-in-on-cells.html |title=Gold nano 'ears' set to listen in on cells |publisher=New Scientist |date=2012-01-11 |access-date=2020-10-23 |author=David Shiga}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Ohlinger |first1=Alexander |last2=Deak |first2=Andras |last3=Lutich |first3=Andrey A. |display-authors=1 |title=Optically Trapped Gold Nanoparticle Enables Listening at the Microscale |journal=Physical Review Letters |publisher=American Physical Society (APS) |volume=108 |issue=1 |date=2012-01-03 |issn=0031-9007 |doi=10.1103/physrevlett.108.018101 |article-number=018101 |pmid=22304294 |bibcode=2012PhRvL.108a8101O }}</ref>
* 14 January – Researchers at the [[University of Cambridge]] repair [[myelin sheath]] damage in ageing mice with [[multiple sclerosis]] by injecting the blood of younger mice into them, reactivating the older mice's regenerative [[stem cell]]s.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.newscientist.com/article/mg21328475.400-ms-damage-washed-away-by-stream-of-young-blood.html |title=MS damage washed away by stream of young blood |publisher=New Scientist |date=2012-01-11 |access-date=2020-10-23}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Ruckh |first1=Julia M. |last2=Zhao |first2=Jing-Wei |last3=Shadrach |first3=Jennifer L. |display-authors=1 |title=Rejuvenation of Regeneration in the Aging Central Nervous System |journal=Cell Stem Cell |publisher=Elsevier BV |volume=10 |issue=1 |year=2012 |issn=1934-5909 |doi=10.1016/j.stem.2011.11.019 |pages=96–103 |pmid=22226359 |pmc=3714794 }}</ref>
* 15 January – Russia's [[Fobos-Grunt]] [[Mars|Martian]] [[sample return]] spacecraft, which became stranded in orbit after a post-launch malfunction in November 2011, re-enters Earth's atmosphere.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/science-environment-16491457 |title=Phobos-Grunt: Failed probe 'falls over Pacific' |publisher=BBC |author=Jonathan Amos |date=2012-01-15 |access-date=2020-10-23}}</ref>
* 18 January
** Astronomers report the discovery of the most distant [[dwarf galaxy]] yet found, approximately 10 billion light-years away.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.csmonitor.com/Science/2012/0118/Dwarf-galaxies-breakthrough-in-bid-to-find-fossils-of-early-universe |title=Dwarf galaxies: breakthrough in bid to find 'fossils' of early universe |work=The Christian Science Monitor|author=Pete Spotts |date=2012-01-18 |access-date=2020-10-23}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Vegetti |first1=S. |last2=Lagattuta |first2=D. J. |last3=McKean |first3=J. P. |display-authors=1 |title=Gravitational detection of a low-mass dark satellite galaxy at cosmological distance |journal=Nature |publisher=Springer Science and Business Media LLC |volume=481 |issue=7381 |year=2012 |issn=0028-0836 |doi=10.1038/nature10669 |pages=341–343 |pmid=22258612 |arxiv=1201.3643 |bibcode=2012Natur.481..341V |s2cid=205227095 }}</ref>
** A British amateur astronomer discovers a new [[Neptune]]-sized exoplanet, just days after the [[BBC]]'s ''Stargazing Live'' program makes a public appeal for volunteers to assist scientists in the search for potential exoplanets. Over 100,000 volunteers are reportedly taking part in the ongoing search.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/science-environment-16612181 |title=Stargazing viewer in planet coup |publisher=BBC |author=Jonathan Amos|date=2012-01-18 |access-date=2020-10-18}}</ref>
** Archaeologists find a novel tulip-shaped fossil, formally named ''Siphusauctum gregarium'', in the [[Middle Cambrian]] [[Burgess Shale]] in the [[Canadian Rockies]]. The 20-centimetre-long creature reportedly possessed a unique [[Filter feeder|filter feeding]] system.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2012/01/120118173659.htm |title=Unusual 'tulip' creature discovered: Lived in the ocean more than 500 million years ago |publisher=Science Daily |date=2012-01-19 |access-date=2020-10-23}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=O'Brien |first1=Lorna J. |last2=Caron |first2=Jean-Bernard |editor-last=Farke |editor-first=Andrew A. |title=A New Stalked Filter-Feeder from the Middle Cambrian Burgess Shale, British Columbia, Canada |journal=PLOS ONE |publisher=Public Library of Science (PLoS) |volume=7 |issue=1 |date=2012-01-18 |issn=1932-6203 |doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0029233 |article-number=e29233 |pmid=22279532 |pmc=3261148 |bibcode=2012PLoSO...729233O |s2cid=3910961 |doi-access=free }}</ref>
** A working [[Semiconductor device fabrication|9-nanometer]] [[transistor]] is developed by [[IBM]] engineers, demonstrating that [[Carbon nanotube|nanotubes]] could serve as a viable alternative to [[silicon]] in future [[nanoelectronic]] devices.<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://www.technologyreview.com/news/426711/smallest-ever-nanotube-transistors-outperform-silicon/ |title=Smallest-Ever Nanotube Transistors Outperform Silicon |first=Katherine |last=Bourzac |date=26 January 2012 |website=MIT Technology Review |access-date=29 June 2021 |archive-date=28 December 2015 |archive-url=https://wayback.archive-it.org/all/20151228135220/http://www.technologyreview.com/news/426711/smallest-ever-nanotube-transistors-outperform-silicon/ |url-status=bot: unknown}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Franklin |first1=Aaron D. |last2=Luisier |first2=Mathieu |last3=Han |first3=Shu-Jen |display-authors=1 |title=Sub-10 nm Carbon Nanotube Transistor |journal=Nano Letters |publisher=American Chemical Society (ACS) |volume=12 |issue=2 |date=2012-01-18 |issn=1530-6984 |doi=10.1021/nl203701g |pages=758–762 |pmid=22260387 |bibcode=2012NanoL..12..758F |s2cid=12194219 }}</ref>
* 19 January
** Austrian researchers develop a [[quantum computer]] capable of performing calculations without revealing any of the data involved, using encoded strings of [[photon]]s designed to appear random. This method of "blind [[quantum cryptography]]" may permit sensitive data to be processed and transferred without any danger of interception or decryption, leading to ultra-secure [[cloud computing]].<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.newscientist.com/article/dn21377-first-secure-quantum-computer-is-blind-to-its-own-bits.html|title=First secure quantum computer is blind to its own bits |publisher=New Scientist |author=Justin Mullins |date=2012-01-19 |access-date=2020-10-23}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Barz |first1=S. |last2=Kashefi |first2=E. |last3=Broadbent |first3=A. |display-authors=1|title=Demonstration of Blind Quantum Computing |journal=Science |volume=335 |issue=6066 |date=2012-01-19 |issn=0036-8075 |doi=10.1126/science.1214707 |pages=303–308 |pmid=22267806 |arxiv=1110.1381 |bibcode=2012Sci...335..303B |s2cid=24363424 }}</ref>
** NASA data shows that in 2011, temperatures in the Arctic rose beyond the record established in 2010 – setting a new record.<ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.skepticalscience.com/New_temperature_record_for_the_Arctic_in_2011.html |title=	New temperature record for the Arctic in 2011 |publisher=Skeptical Science |author=Neven |date=2012-01-27 |access-date=2020-10-23}}</ref>
* 20 January – [[Virologist]]s agree to a temporarily hiatus on experiments on the [[H5N1]] [[influenza]] virus, due to fears that an airborne strain of the lethal virus could be used by [[bioterrorism|bioterrorists]].<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.newscientist.com/blogs/shortsharpscience/2012/01/bird-flu-researchers-stand-dow.html |title=Bird flu researchers stand down for 60 days |publisher=New Scientist |author=Debora MacKenzie |date=2012-01-20 |access-date=2020-10-23}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Fouchier |first1=R. A. M. |last2=Garcia-Sastre |first2=A. |last3=Kawaoka |first3=Y. |display-authors=1|title=Pause on Avian Flu Transmission Research |journal=Science |publisher=American Association for the Advancement of Science (AAAS) |volume=335 |issue=6067 |date=2012-01-20 |issn=0036-8075 |doi=10.1126/science.1219412 |pages=400–401 |pmid=22282787 |pmc=3812248 }}</ref>
* 22 January
** American researchers report that [[nanoparticles]] can be successfully engineered to mimic part of the body's [[immune system]], improving its response to vaccines.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/health-16654182 |title=Nanoparticle trick 'boosts body's vaccine response' |publisher=BBC |date=2012-01-22 |access-date=2020-10-23}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=St. John |first1=Ashley L. |last2=Chan |first2=Cheryl Y. |last3=Staats |first3=Herman F. | display-authors=1 |title=Synthetic mast-cell granules as adjuvants to promote and polarize immunity in lymph nodes |journal=Nature Materials |publisher=Springer Science and Business Media LLC |volume=11 |issue=3 |date=2012-01-22 |issn=1476-1122 |doi=10.1038/nmat3222 |pages=250–257 |pmid=22266469 |pmc=3749235 |bibcode=2012NatMa..11..250S }}</ref>
** An international team of scientists concludes that anthropogenic [[CO2 emissions|CO<sub>2</sub> emissions]] over the last 100 to 200 years have already raised [[Ocean acidification|ocean acidity]] far beyond the range of natural variations.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2012/01/120122152542.htm |title=Unprecedented, human-made trends in ocean's acidity |publisher=Science Daily |date=2012-01-26 |access-date=2020-10-23}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Friedrich |first1=T. |last2=Timmermann |first2=A. |last3=Abe-Ouchi |first3=A. |display-authors=1|title=Detecting regional anthropogenic trends in ocean acidification against natural variability |journal=Nature Climate Change |publisher=Springer Science and Business Media LLC |volume=2 |issue=3 |date=2012-01-22 |issn=1758-678X |doi=10.1038/nclimate1372 |pages=167–171 |bibcode=2012NatCC...2..167F }}</ref>
[[File:Humanstemcell.JPG|thumb|right|250px|23 January 2012: [[stem cell therapy]] is successfully used to ease the symptoms of [[blindness]] in human volunteers (human [[embryonic stem cell]] shown).]]
* 23 January
** South Korean scientists develop [[touchscreen]]s that can recognise the existence and concentration of [[DNA]] molecules placed on them. The invention could allow the development of [[smartphone]]s with the ability to diagnose users' medical conditions.<ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.abc.net.au/science/articles/2012/01/23/3413821.htm |title=Future phones could measure your health |publisher=ABC Science |author=Hyunyoung Yi and Iktae Park |date=2012-01-23 |access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Won |first1=Byoung Yeon |last2=Park |first2=Hyun Gyu |title=A Touchscreen as a Biomolecule Detection Platform |journal=Angewandte Chemie International Edition |publisher=Wiley |volume=51 |issue=3 |date=2011-10-26 |issn=1433-7851 |doi=10.1002/anie.201105986 |pages=748–751 |pmid=22031359 }}</ref>
** ''[[The Lancet]]'' reports that a human medical trial of [[embryonic stem cell]]s successfully eased a degenerative form of [[blindness]] in two volunteers, and showed no signs of any adverse effects.<ref>{{cite news |url=http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-01-stem-cells-aid-vision-people.html |title=Study: Stem cells may aid vision in blind people |publisher=Medical Xpress |date=2012-01-23 |access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Schwartz |first1=Steven D |last2=Hubschman |first2=Jean-Pierre |last3=Heilwell |first3=Gad |display-authors=1|title=Embryonic stem cell trials for macular degeneration: a preliminary report |journal=The Lancet |publisher=Elsevier BV |volume=379 |issue=9817 |year=2012 |issn=0140-6736 |doi=10.1016/s0140-6736(12)60028-2 |pages=713–720 |pmid=22281388 |s2cid=2230787 }}</ref>
** Brain scans of people under the influence of [[psilocybin]], the active ingredient in [[magic mushrooms]], have given scientists the most detailed picture to date of how [[psychedelic drugs]] work.<ref>{{cite news |url=http://www3.imperial.ac.uk/newsandeventspggrp/imperialcollege/newssummary/news_24-1-2012-10-39-58#fni-3 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120503223242/http://www3.imperial.ac.uk/newsandeventspggrp/imperialcollege/newssummary/news_24-1-2012-10-39-58 |archive-date=2012-05-03 |title=Magic mushrooms' effects illuminated in brain imaging studies |publisher=Imperial College London |date=2012-01-24 |access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Carhart-Harris |first1=R. L. |last2=Erritzoe |first2=D. |last3=Williams |first3=T. |display-authors=1|title=Neural correlates of the psychedelic state as determined by fMRI studies with psilocybin |journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences |volume=109 |issue=6 |date=2012-01-23 |issn=0027-8424 |doi=10.1073/pnas.1119598109 |pages=2138–2143 |pmid=22308440 |pmc=3277566 |doi-access=free }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Carhart-Harris |first1=R. L. |last2=Leech |first2=R. |last3=Williams |first3=T. M. |display-authors=1|title=Implications for psychedelic-assisted psychotherapy: functional magnetic resonance imaging study with psilocybin |journal=British Journal of Psychiatry |publisher=Royal College of Psychiatrists |volume=200 |issue=3 |year=2012 |issn=0007-1250 |doi=10.1192/bjp.bp.111.103309 |pages=238–244 |pmid=22282432 |doi-access=free }}</ref>
* 24 January
** Earth is struck by the largest [[coronal mass ejection|solar storm]] since 2005, creating huge [[aurora (astronomy)|aurora]]e and potentially interfering with satellite communications worldwide.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/science-environment-16701407 |title=Solar storm's effects to lash Earth until Wednesday |publisher=BBC News |author=Paul Rincon |date=2012-01-24 |access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref>
** A nest of [[dinosaur eggs]] 100 million years older than the previous oldest site is found in [[South Africa]]. The fossils are of the [[prosauropod]] species ''[[Massospondylus]]'', a relative of the long-necked [[sauropod]]s.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/science-environment-16697954 |title=Oldest dinosaur nest site found |publisher=BBC News |date=2012-01-24 |access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Reisz |first1=R. R. |last2=Evans |first2=D. C. |last3=Roberts |first3=E. M. |display-authors=1|title=Oldest known dinosaurian nesting site and reproductive biology of the Early Jurassic sauropodomorph Massospondylus |journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences |volume=109 |issue=7 |date=2012-01-24 |issn=0027-8424 |doi=10.1073/pnas.1109385109 |pages=2428–2433 |pmid=22308330 |pmc=3289328 |bibcode=2012PNAS..109.2428R |doi-access=free }}</ref>
* 25 January
** University of Washington scientists report that injecting [[sulfate]] particles into the [[stratosphere]] will not fully offset [[climate change]].<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2012/01/120125142212.htm |title=Injecting sulfate particles into stratosphere won't fully offset climate change |publisher=Science Daily |date=2012-01-25 |access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=McCusker |first1=Kelly E. |last2=Battisti |first2=David S. |last3=Bitz |first3=Cecilia M. |display-authors=1 |title=The Climate Response to Stratospheric Sulfate Injections and Implications for Addressing Climate Emergencies |journal=Journal of Climate |publisher=American Meteorological Society |volume=25 |issue=9 |year=2012 |issn=0894-8755 |doi=10.1175/jcli-d-11-00183.1 |pages=3096–3116 |bibcode=2012JCli...25.3096M |s2cid=15517515 |doi-access=free }}</ref>
** A study in Japan finds that [[green tea]] can significantly reduce disability in the elderly, likely due to its [[antioxidant]] content.<ref>{{cite news |url=http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-02-green-tea-disability-elderly.html |title=Green tea found to reduce disability in the elderly |publisher=MedicalXpress |author=Bob Yirka |date=2012-02-07 |access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Tomata |first1=Yasutake |last2=Kakizaki |first2=Masako |last3=Nakaya |first3=Naoki |display-authors=1|title=Green tea consumption and the risk of incident functional disability in elderly Japanese: the Ohsaki Cohort 2006 Study |journal=The American Journal of Clinical Nutrition |publisher=Oxford University Press (OUP) |volume=95 |issue=3 |date=2012-01-25 |issn=0002-9165 |doi=10.3945/ajcn.111.023200 |pages=732–739 |pmid=22277550 |pmc=3278248 }}</ref>
* 26 January – American researchers successfully "[[Cloak of invisibility|cloak]]" a three-dimensional object, making it invisible from all angles, for the first time. However, the demonstration works only for waves in the [[microwave]] region of the [[electromagnetic spectrum]].<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/science-environment-16726609 |title='Cloaking' a 3-D object from all angles demonstrated |publisher=BBC News |author=Jason Palmer |date=2012-01-26 |access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Rainwater |first1=D |last2=Kerkhoff |first2=A |last3=Melin |first3=K |display-authors=1|title=Experimental verification of three-dimensional plasmonic cloaking in free-space |journal=New Journal of Physics |volume=14 |issue=1 |date=2012-01-25 |issn=1367-2630 |doi=10.1088/1367-2630/14/1/013054 |article-number=013054 |arxiv=1107.3740 |bibcode=2012NJPh...14a3054R |s2cid=54629758 }}</ref>
[[File:Campo12Foto 2.JPG|thumb|right|250px|27 January 2012: the most detailed 3D image of the [[Amazon rainforest]] yet produced is published.]]
* 27 January
** An international team of scientists reports that [[graphene]], already widely known for its conductive properties, is also able to selectively filter gases and liquids. The material could thus potentially find use in industrial [[distillation]] and water purification.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/science-environment-16747208 |title=Miracle material graphene can distil booze, says study |publisher=BBC News |date=2012-01-27 |access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.theregister.co.uk/2012/01/27/graphene_blocks_liquids_gases/ |title=Boffins make graphene micro-distillery |publisher=The Register |author=Brid-Aine Parnell |date=2012-01-27 |access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Nair |first1=R. R. |last2=Wu |first2=H. A. |last3=Jayaram |first3=P. N. |display-authors=1|title=Unimpeded Permeation of Water Through Helium-Leak-Tight Graphene-Based Membranes |journal=Science |volume=335 |issue=6067 |date=2012-01-26 |issn=0036-8075 |doi=10.1126/science.1211694 |pages=442–444 |pmid=22282806 |arxiv=1112.3488 |bibcode=2012Sci...335..442N |s2cid=15204080 }}</ref>
** A study published in the journal ''Carcinogenesis'' shows that in both cell lines and mouse models, [[grape seed extract]] (GSE) kills head and neck cancer cells, while leaving healthy cells unharmed.<ref>{{cite news |url=http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-01-grape-seed-neck-cancer-cells.html |title=Grape seed extract kills head and neck cancer cells, leaves healthy cells unharmed |publisher=MedicalXpress |date=2012-01-27 |access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Shrotriya |first1=S. |last2=Deep |first2=G. |last3=Gu |first3=M. |display-authors=1|title=Generation of reactive oxygen species by grape seed extract causes irreparable DNA damage leading to G2/M arrest and apoptosis selectively in head and neck squamous cell carcinoma cells |journal=Carcinogenesis |publisher=Oxford University Press (OUP) |volume=33 |issue=4 |date=2012-01-19 |issn=0143-3334 |doi=10.1093/carcin/bgs019 |pages=848–858 |pmid=22266465 |pmc=3324440 }}</ref>
** Using an airborne [[LIDAR]] system, scientists produce the most detailed 3D image of the [[Amazon rainforest]] yet recorded, allowing the accurate measurement of the rainforest's ecosystem and rate of [[deforestation]].<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.theguardian.com/environment/2012/jan/27/amazon-rainforest-map-biodiversity-detail |title=Amazon rainforest mapped in unprecedented detail |work=The Guardian |author=Dan Collyns |date=2012-01-27 |access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref>
** [[2012 BX34|2012 BX<sub>34</sub>]], an asteroid between {{convert|8|and|11|m|ft}} across, passes within 60,000 kilometres of the Earth, performing one of the closest asteroid flybys yet recorded.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/science-environment-16756450 |title=Asteroid makes near-miss fly-by |publisher=BBC News |date=2012-01-27 |access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |url=http://echo.jpl.nasa.gov/asteroids/2012BX34/2012BX34_planning.html |title=2012 BX34 Goldstone Radar Observations Planning |publisher=NASA Jet Propulsion Laboratory}}</ref>
** British animators develop a new [[algorithm]]ic method of creating highly realistic CGI trees, allowing films and video games to easily display realistic 3D foliage.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.newscientist.com/article/mg21328495.600-virtual-trees-sway-in-wind-just-like-the-real-thing.html |title=Virtual trees sway in wind just like the real thing |publisher=New Scientist |author=Paul Marks |date=2012-01-25 |access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Li |first1=Chuan |last2=Deussen |first2=Oliver |last3=Song |first3=Yi-Zhe |display-authors=1|title=Modeling and generating moving trees from video |journal=ACM Transactions on Graphics |publisher=Association for Computing Machinery (ACM) |volume=30 |issue=6 |year=2011 |issn=0730-0301 |doi=10.1145/2070781.2024161 |pages=1–12 |url=http://opus.bath.ac.uk/28064/1/Li_ACM%2DTrans%2DGraphics_2011_30_6_127.pdf }}</ref>
* 29 January – Using [[stem cells]] generated from patients with [[schizophrenia]], [[bipolar depression]] and other [[mental illnesses]], scientists at the [[University of Edinburgh]] create [[neurones]] with brain tissue genetically identical to the person's brain. The breakthrough could allow new treatments for mental illnesses to be accurately tested without endangering patients.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.theguardian.com/science/2012/jan/29/brain-cloning-breakthrough-mental-illness |title=Cloning scientists create human brain cells |work=The Guardian |author=Robin McKie |date=2012-01-28 |access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref>
* 30 January
** A [[United Nations]] report warns that time is running out to ensure there is enough food, water and energy for a rapidly rising [[world population]]. By 2030, the world will need at least 50 percent more food, 45 percent more energy and 30 percent more water, according to estimates.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.reuters.com/article/us-un-development-idUSTRE80T10520120130 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120201012104/https://www.reuters.com/article/2012/01/30/us-un-development-idUSTRE80T10520120130 |archive-date=2012-02-01 |url-status=live |title=World lacks enough food, fuel as population soars: U.N. |work=Reuters|author=Nina Chestney |date=2012-01-30 |access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref>
** The British [[Royal Navy]] begins development of a new anti-missile defence system, the Sea Ceptor, capable of intercepting and destroying [[supersonic]] missiles within an area of {{convert|500|mi2|km2}}. The system is likely to enter service by 2017.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/newsbeat-16785483 |title=Royal Navy reveals new supersonic anti-missile system |publisher=BBC |date=2012-01-30 |access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref>
** American researchers report that [[ultrasound]] waves can be used effectively to kill [[sperm]], potentially offering a new [[male contraceptive]] method.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Tsuruta |first1=James K |last2=Dayton |first2=Paul A |last3=Gallippi |first3=Caterina M |display-authors=1|title=Therapeutic ultrasound as a potential male contraceptive: power, frequency and temperature required to deplete rat testes of meiotic cells and epididymides of sperm determined using a commercially available system |journal=Reproductive Biology and Endocrinology |publisher=Springer Science and Business Media LLC |volume=10 |issue=1 |year=2012 |issn=1477-7827 |doi=10.1186/1477-7827-10-7 |page=7 |pmid=22289508 |pmc=3340307 |doi-access=free }}</ref>
** [[Ozone]] from anthropogenic [[air pollution]] in North America leads to the annual loss of 1.2 million tonnes of [[wheat]] in Europe alone, according to a study published by British universities.<ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.physorg.com/news/2012-01-food-crops-pollution-continents.html |title=Food crops damaged by pollution crossing continents |publisher=PhysOrg |date=2012-01-30 |access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Hollaway |first1=M. J. |last2=Arnold |first2=S. R. |last3=Challinor |first3=A. J. |display-authors=1|title=Intercontinental trans-boundary contributions to ozone-induced crop yield losses in the Northern Hemisphere |journal=Biogeosciences |publisher=Copernicus GmbH |volume=9 |issue=1 |date=2012-01-16 |issn=1726-4189 |doi=10.5194/bg-9-271-2012 |pages=271–292 |bibcode=2012BGeo....9..271H |doi-access=free |hdl=10568/25147 |hdl-access=free }}</ref>
** A NASA study reports that changes in [[solar flare|solar activity]] cannot be responsible for the current period of [[global warming]]. The sun's total [[solar cycle#Solar irradiance|solar irradiance]] has in recent years dipped to the lowest levels recorded during the satellite era.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2012/01/120130172611.htm |title=Earth's energy budget remained out of balance despite unusually low solar activity, study finds |publisher=ScienceDaily |date=2012-01-30 |access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Hansen |first1=J. |last2=Sato |first2=M. |last3=Kharecha |first3=P. |display-authors=1|title=Earth's energy imbalance and implications |journal=Atmospheric Chemistry and Physics |publisher=Copernicus GmbH |volume=11 |issue=24 |date=2011-12-22 |issn=1680-7324 |doi=10.5194/acp-11-13421-2011 |pages=13421–13449 |arxiv=1105.1140 |bibcode=2011ACP....1113421H |s2cid=16937940 |doi-access=free }}</ref>
** According to [[genetics|genetic studies]], [[modern humans]] seem to have mated with "at least two groups" of [[Archaic humans|ancient humans]]: [[Neanderthals]] and [[Denisovans]].<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2012/01/31/science/gains-in-dna-are-speeding-research-into-human-origins.html |title=DNA Turning Human Story into a Tell-All |work=The New York Times |author=Alanna Mitchell |date=2012-01-30 |access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Reich |first1=David |last2=Green |first2=Richard E. |last3=Kircher |first3=Martin |display-authors=1|title=Genetic history of an archaic hominin group from Denisova Cave in Siberia |journal=Nature |publisher=Springer Science and Business Media LLC |volume=468 |issue=7327 |year=2010 |issn=0028-0836 |doi=10.1038/nature09710 |pages=1053–1060 |pmid=21179161 |pmc=4306417 |bibcode=2010Natur.468.1053R }}</ref>
[[File:Superior temporal gyrus.png|thumb|right|250px|31 January 2012: American scientists demonstrate a method of decoding human thoughts by studying the [[superior temporal gyrus]] (indicated).]]
* 31 January
** American scientists successfully demonstrate a method of decoding thoughts by studying activity in the human brain's [[superior temporal gyrus]], which is involved in linguistic processing. Using this method, a device which reads and transmits the thoughts of brain-damaged patients could become a reality in the future.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/science/science-news/9051909/Mind-reading-device-could-become-reality.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120201081520/https://www.telegraph.co.uk/science/science-news/9051909/Mind-reading-device-could-become-reality.html |archive-date=2012-02-01 |work=The Telegraph |title=Mind-reading device could become reality |author=Nick Collins |date=2012-01-31 |access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/science-environment-16811042 |title=Science decodes 'internal voices' |publisher=BBC News |author=Jason Palmer |date=2012-02-01 |access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Pasley |first1=Brian N. |last2=David |first2=Stephen V. |last3=Mesgarani |first3=Nima |display-authors=1|editor-last=Zatorre |editor-first=Robert |title=Reconstructing Speech from Human Auditory Cortex |journal=PLOS Biology |publisher=Public Library of Science (PLoS) |volume=10 |issue=1 |date=2012-01-31 |issn=1545-7885 |doi=10.1371/journal.pbio.1001251 |article-number=e1001251 |pmid=22303281 |pmc=3269422 |doi-access=free }}</ref>
** [[Integrated circuit|Microchip]] designer [[AMD]] launches its [[Comparison of AMD graphics processing units#Southern Islands .28HD 7xxx.29 Series|Radeon HD 7950]] graphics card, based on a [[28 nanometer]] manufacturing process – a more advanced [[die shrink]] of the current [[32 nanometer]] standard.<ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.theinquirer.net/inquirer/news/2142608/amd-launches-radeon-hd-7950-graphics-card |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120203000544/http://www.theinquirer.net/inquirer/news/2142608/amd-launches-radeon-hd-7950-graphics-card |archive-date=2012-02-03 |url-status=unfit |title=AMD launches its Radeon HD 7950 graphics card |publisher=The Inquirer |author=Chris Martin |date=2012-01-31 |access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref>
** [[Poyang Lake]], China's largest freshwater lake, has almost completely dried up due to a combination of severe [[drought]] and the impact of the recently built [[Three Gorges Dam]].<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.theguardian.com/environment/2012/jan/31/china-freshwater-lake-dries-up |title=China's largest freshwater lake dries up |work=The Guardian |author=Harold Thibault |date=2012-01-31 |access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref>

===February===
[[File:Aerial View of the VLTI with Tunnels Superimposed.jpg|thumb|right|250px|3 February 2012: the [[Very Large Telescope]] array enters operation in northern [[Chile]].]]
* 1 February – Researchers report that the eruption of [[supervolcano]]es could be predicted several decades before the event by detecting the seismic and chemical signs of a massive [[magma]] buildup.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/science-environment-16834570 |title=Mega volcanoes 'may be predicted' |publisher=BBC News |author=Neil Bowdler |date=2012-02-01 |access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Druitt |first1=T. H. |last2=Costa |first2=F. |last3=Deloule |first3=E. |display-authors=1|title=Decadal to monthly timescales of magma transfer and reservoir growth at a caldera volcano |journal=Nature |publisher=Springer Science and Business Media LLC |volume=482 |issue=7383 |year=2012 |issn=0028-0836 |doi=10.1038/nature10706 |pages=77–80 |pmid=22297973 |bibcode=2012Natur.482...77D |hdl=10356/94274 |s2cid=4422180 |url=https://hal-insu.archives-ouvertes.fr/insu-00666208/file/DRUITT_whole_paper.pdf }}</ref>
* 2 February
** The [[European Commission]] issues a 225-million-[[euro]] (US$330 million) contract to an Anglo-German consortium for eight additional satellites to expand Europe's [[Galileo (navigation)|Galileo]] satellite navigation system.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/science-environment-16845557 |title=More Galileo satellites ordered |publisher=BBC News |author=Jonathan Amos |date=2012-02-02 |access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref>
** Astronomers report the discovery of a large [[exoplanet]] orbiting within the [[habitable zone]] of a star 22 light-years distant. This is the fourth potentially life-supporting exoplanet discovered since May 2011.<ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.sfgate.com/science/article/Super-Earth-planet-spurs-hope-for-billions-more-2964117.php |title='Super-Earth' planet spurs hope for billions more |work=San Francisco Chronicle |author=David Perlman |date=2012-02-02 |access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref>
** Researchers reportedly create the world's thinnest pane of glass, a sheet of silicon and oxygen just three atoms wide. The glass formed in an accidental reaction when the scientists were synthesizing [[graphene]] on [[copper]]-covered [[quartz]].<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.sciencemag.org/sciencenow/2012/02/scienceshot-two-dimensional-glass.html?ref=hp |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120205093201/http://news.sciencemag.org/sciencenow/2012/02/scienceshot-two-dimensional-glass.html?ref=hp |archive-date=2012-02-05 |publisher=ScienceMag |title=ScienceShot: Two-Dimensional Glass |author=Katherine McAlpine |date=2012-02-02 |access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Huang |first1=Pinshane Y. |last2=Kurasch |first2=Simon |last3=Srivastava |first3=Anchal |display-authors=1|title=Direct Imaging of a Two-Dimensional Silica Glass on Graphene |journal=Nano Letters |publisher=American Chemical Society (ACS) |volume=12 |issue=2 |date=2012-01-26 |issn=1530-6984 |doi=10.1021/nl204423x |pages=1081–1086 |pmid=22268818 |bibcode=2012NanoL..12.1081H }}</ref>
* 3 February
** The [[European Southern Observatory]] successfully activates its [[Very Large Telescope]] (VLT) by linking four existing optical telescopes to operate as a single device. The linked VLT is the largest optical telescope yet built, with a combined mirror diameter of {{convert|130|m|ft}}.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/science-environment-16869022 |title=Four telescope link-up creates world's largest mirror |publisher=BBC News |author=Katia Moskvitch |date=2012-02-03 |access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref>
** Physicists at Germany's [[Max Planck Institute]] unveil a [[microscope]] that can image living brain cells as they function inside a living animal.<ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.physorg.com/news/2012-02-renowned-physicist-microscope-peer-brain.html |title=Renowned physicist invents microscope that can peer at living brain cells |publisher=PhysOrg |author=Bob Yirka |date=2020-02-03 |access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Berning |first1=S. |last2=Willig |first2=K. I. |last3=Steffens |first3=H. |display-authors=1|title=Nanoscopy in a Living Mouse Brain |journal=Science |publisher=American Association for the Advancement of Science (AAAS) |volume=335 |issue=6068 |date=2012-02-02 |issn=0036-8075 |doi=10.1126/science.1215369 |page=551 |pmid=22301313 |bibcode=2012Sci...335..551B |hdl=11858/00-001M-0000-000F-4143-6 |s2cid=1642489 |hdl-access=free }}</ref>
** American scientists demonstrate a medical procedure that may allow patients with [[nerve]] damage to recover within weeks, rather than months or years. The procedure makes use of a cellular mechanism similar to that which repairs nerve [[axon]]s in [[invertebrate]]s.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2012/02/120203092423.htm |title=New procedure repairs severed nerves in minutes, restoring limb use in days or weeks |publisher=Science Daily |date=2012-02-03 |access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Bittner |first1=G.D. |last2=Keating |first2=C.P. |last3=Kane |first3=J.R. |display-authors=1|title=Rapid, effective, and long-lasting behavioral recovery produced by microsutures, methylene blue, and polyethylene glycol after completely cutting rat sciatic nerves |journal=Journal of Neuroscience Research |publisher=Wiley |volume=90 |issue=5 |date=2012-02-03 |issn=0360-4012 |doi=10.1002/jnr.23023 |pages=967–980 |pmid=22302646 |s2cid=14781154 }}</ref>
** [[MIT]] researchers develop high-temperature [[photonic crystal]]s capable of efficiently converting heat to electricity, potentially allowing the creation of pocket-sized microreactors with ten times the efficiency and lifespan of current commercial batteries. As photonic crystals are already a relatively mature technology, the new invention could be commercialised in as little as two years.<ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.extremetech.com/extreme/116853-mits-photonic-crystals-lead-towards-a-nuclear-reactor-in-every-gadget |title=MIT's photonic crystals lead towards nuclear batteries everywhere |publisher=ExtremeTech |author=Sebastian Anthony |date=2012-02-03 |access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref>
** A ''[[The Lancet|Lancet]]'' study reports that global [[malaria]] deaths may be badly underestimated, giving a revised 2010 malaria death toll of 1.24 million. By contrast, the [[World Health Organization]] estimated that 655,000 people died of malaria in 2010.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/health-16854026 |title=Malaria deaths hugely underestimated – Lancet study |publisher=BBC News |author=Neil Bowdler |date=2012-02-03 |access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Murray |first1=Christopher JL |last2=Rosenfeld |first2=Lisa C |last3=Lim |first3=Stephen S |display-authors=1|title=Global malaria mortality between 1980 and 2010: a systematic analysis |journal=The Lancet |publisher=Elsevier BV |volume=379 |issue=9814 |year=2012 |issn=0140-6736 |doi=10.1016/s0140-6736(12)60034-8 |pages=413–431 |pmid=22305225 |bibcode=2012Lanc..379..413M |s2cid=46171431 }}</ref>
* 4 February – Dutch doctors successfully fit an 83-year-old woman with an artificial jaw made using a [[3D printing|3D printer]]. This operation, the first of its kind, could herald a new era of accurate, patient-tailored artificial transplants.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/technology-16907104 |title=Transplant jaw made by 3D printer claimed as first |publisher=BBC News |date=2012-03-08 |access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref>
[[File:ORDbot quantum.jpg|thumb|right|250px|4 February 2012: Dutch doctors successfully fit the first artificial jaw made with a [[3D printing|3D printer]] (ORDbot Quantum 3D printer pictured).]]
* 6 February
** After nearly 20 years of intermittent drilling, Russian scientists reportedly break through to the surface of the subterranean [[Lake Vostok]], buried {{convert|2.5|mi|km}} under the [[Antarctic]] ice. The lake, which has not been uncovered for over 15 million years, may harbour a unique prehistoric ecosystem.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.theguardian.com/world/2012/feb/06/russian-scientists-drill-antarctic-lake |title=Russian scientists drill into Antarctic lake sealed off for 15 million years |work=The Guardian |author=Alok Jha |date=2012-02-06 |access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/national/health-science/scientists-close-to-entering-vostok-antarcticas-biggest-subglacial-lake/2012/01/27/gIQAbGX0fQ_story.html |title=Scientists close to entering Vostock, Antarctica's biggest subglacial lake |newspaper=The Washington Post|author=Marc Kaufman |date=2012-01-31 |access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref>
** A team of engineers and biologists develop a working [[write once read many|WORM]] computer memory out of [[salmon]] DNA molecules by combining the DNA with [[silver nanoparticle]]s.<ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.extremetech.com/extreme/117191-computer-memory-made-out-of-salmon-dna |title=Computer memory made out of salmon DNA |publisher=ExtremeTech |author=Sebastian Anthony |date=2012-02-06 |access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Hung |first1=Yu-Chueh |last2=Hsu |first2=Wei-Ting |last3=Lin |first3=Ting-Yu |display-authors=1|title=Photoinduced write-once read-many-times memory device based on DNA biopolymer nanocomposite |journal=Applied Physics Letters |publisher=AIP Publishing |volume=99 |issue=25 |date=2011-12-19 |issn=0003-6951 |doi=10.1063/1.3671153 |page=253301 |bibcode=2011ApPhL..99y3301H }}</ref>
* 7 February
** Scientists report that rapid declines in some British and European [[ladybird]] species are being caused by the spread of the invasive [[Harmonia axyridis|harlequin]] species.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/science-environment-16916726 |title=Ladybird decline driven by 'invading' harlequin |publisher=BBC News |author=Richard Black |date=2012-02-07 |access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Roy |first1=Helen E. |last2=Adriaens |first2=Tim |last3=Isaac |first3=Nick J. B. |display-authors=1|title=Invasive alien predator causes rapid declines of native European ladybirds |journal=Diversity and Distributions |publisher=Wiley |volume=18 |issue=7 |date=2012-02-07 |issn=1366-9516 |doi=10.1111/j.1472-4642.2012.00883.x |pages=717–725 |bibcode=2012DivDi..18..717R |doi-access=free }}</ref>
** The entire [[genome]] of an extinct species of human – the 40,000-year-old [[Denisova hominin]] – has been decoded from a fossil.<ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.physorg.com/news/2012-02-entire-genome-extinct-human-decoded.html|title=Entire genome of extinct human decoded from fossil |publisher=PhysOrg |date=2012-02-07 |access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref>
* 8 February – NASA data reveals that the total land ice lost from [[Greenland]], [[Antarctica]] and Earth's [[glacier]]s and ice caps between 2003 and 2010 totalled about 4.3 trillion tons (1,000 cubic miles), adding about {{convert|0.5|in|mm|abbr=off|sp=us}} to global sea levels. Such a quantity of ice would be sufficient to cover the entire United States to a depth of {{convert|1.5|ft|m|abbr=off|sp=us}}.<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://www.jpl.nasa.gov/news/news.cfm?release=2012-036#1 |title=(NASA/JPL) |website=[[Jet Propulsion Laboratory]] |archive-date=16 February 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120216011615/http://www.jpl.nasa.gov//news//news.cfm?release=2012-036#1 }}</ref>
* 9 February – Researchers at [[Case Western Reserve University]] discover that bexarotene, a drug normally used to treat [[skin cancer]], can quickly reverse the symptoms of [[Alzheimer's disease]] in mice, removing over 50% of the disease's trademark [[amyloid plaque]] from the brain within 72 hours.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://edition.cnn.com/2012/02/09/health/us-cancer-drug-alzheimers/index.html?hpt=hp_t3 |title=Skin cancer drug reverses Alzheimer's in mice |publisher=CNN|author=Elizabeth Cohen and Senior Medical Correspondent |date=2012-02-09 |access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Cramer |first1=P. E. |last2=Cirrito |first2=J. R. |last3=Wesson |first3=D. W. |display-authors=1|title=ApoE-Directed Therapeutics Rapidly Clear -Amyloid and Reverse Deficits in AD Mouse Models |journal=Science |publisher=American Association for the Advancement of Science (AAAS) |volume=335 |issue=6075 |date=2012-02-09 |issn=0036-8075 |doi=10.1126/science.1217697 |pages=1503–1506 |pmid=22323736 |pmc=3651582 }}</ref>
* 10 February – Scientists at the [[University of California, San Diego]] report the creation of the tiniest [[telecommunications]] [[laser]] yet built, just 200 nanometers wide. The highly efficient nanolaser could be used to develop [[optical computer]]s and ultra-high-resolution imaging systems.<ref>{{cite news |title=Tiniest Telecommunications Laser Ever Made, 200 Nanometres Wide |url=http://www.popsci.com.au/technology/tiniest-telecommunications-laser-ever-made-200-nanometres-wide |publisher=PopSci |date=2012-02-10 |access-date=2020-10-24 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120419125741/http://www.popsci.com.au/technology/tiniest-telecommunications-laser-ever-made-200-nanometres-wide |archive-date=2012-04-19}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Khajavikhan |first1=M.|author1-link=Mercedeh Khajavikhan |last2=Simic |first2=A. |last3=Katz |first3=M. |display-authors=1|title=Thresholdless nanoscale coaxial lasers |journal=Nature |publisher=Springer Science and Business Media LLC |volume=482 |issue=7384 |year=2012 |issn=0028-0836 |doi=10.1038/nature10840 |pages=204–207 |pmid=22318604 |arxiv=1108.4749 |bibcode=2012Natur.482..204K |s2cid=4431841 }}</ref>
* 13 February
** A new UN report warns that 24 percent of global land area has declined in productivity over the past 25 years due to unsustainable land-use, and [[soil erosion]] rates are about 100 times greater than nature can replenish.<ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.upi.com/Science_News/2012/02/13/UN-highlights-pressing-green-issues/UPI-47421329167681/ |title=U.N. highlights pressing green issues |publisher=UPI |date=2012-02-13 |access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.unep.org/yearbook/2012/ |title=United Nations Environment Programme |publisher=UNEP |access-date=2020-10-24 |archive-url=http://webarchive.loc.gov/all/20150208164726/http%3A//www.unep.org/yearbook/2012/ |archive-date=2015-02-08 }}</ref>
** The [[European Space Agency]] successfully conducts the maiden launch of its new [[Vega (rocket)|Vega]] rocket, transporting several satellites into orbit, including the first [[Poland|Polish]], [[Hungary|Hungarian]] and [[Romania]]n satellites.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/science/space/9079859/Vega-rocket-launches-first-European-satellites-into-space.html |title=Vega rocket launches first European satellites into space |work=The Telegraph |date=2012-02-13 |access-date=2020-10-24 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120216124246/https://www.telegraph.co.uk/science/space/9079859/Vega-rocket-launches-first-European-satellites-into-space.html |archive-date=2012-02-16 }}</ref>
** [[BAE Systems]] engineers unveil a [[carbon-fiber]]-based [[structural battery]] capable of being integrated into a device's framework, reducing weight while maintaining structural strength and power capacity.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/technology-17014808 |title=BAE provides details of 'structural battery' technology |publisher=BBC News |date=2012-03-08 |access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref>
* 14 February – In a groundbreaking human trial, American scientists report that damaged heart tissue in [[heart attack]] patients can be repaired with infusions of the patient's own stem cells. The treatment halved the amount of extant scar tissue within a year.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/health-17012688 |title=Stem cells used to 'heal' heart attack scars |publisher=BBC News |author=James Gallagher |date=2012-02-14 |access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Makkar |first1=Raj R |last2=Smith |first2=Rachel R |last3=Cheng |first3=Ke |display-authors=1|title=Intracoronary cardiosphere-derived cells for heart regeneration after myocardial infarction (CADUCEUS): a prospective, randomised phase 1 trial |journal=The Lancet |publisher=Elsevier BV |volume=379 |issue=9819 |year=2012 |issn=0140-6736 |doi=10.1016/s0140-6736(12)60195-0 |pages=895–904 |pmid=22336189 |pmc=4326004 }}</ref>
[[File:Autonomous TT.jpg|thumb|right|250px|15 February 2012: [[Nevada]] becomes the first US state to release official regulations for the public testing of [[autonomous cars]] (prototype autonomous [[Audi]] pictured).]]
* 15 February – [[Nevada]] becomes the first US state to allow the testing of [[autonomous car|autonomous vehicles]] on US public roadways.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.dmvnv.com/news/12001-regulations-for-self-driving-cars.htm |title=Regulations Clear the Road for Self-driving Cars |publisher=Nevada Department of Motor Vehicles |date=2012-02-15 |access-date=2020-10-24 |archive-date=2012-05-20 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120520033128/http://www.dmvnv.com/news/12001-regulations-for-self-driving-cars.htm }}</ref>
* 16 February – The speed at which someone [[walking|walks]] may predict their likelihood of developing [[dementia]] later in life, according to researchers in the US.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/health-17028712 |title=Slow walking 'predicts dementia' |publisher=BBC News |date=2012-02-16 |access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | last1=Camargo | first1=E. | last2=Beiser | first2=A. | author2-link=Alexa Beiser | last3=Tan | first3=Z. | display-authors=1| title=Walking Speed, Handgrip Strength and Risk of Dementia and Stroke: The Framingham Offspring Study (S24.003) | journal=Neurology | publisher=Ovid Technologies (Wolters Kluwer Health) | volume=78 | issue=Meeting Abstracts 1 | date=2012-04-22 | issn=0028-3878 | doi=10.1212/wnl.78.1_meetingabstracts.s24.003 | article-number=S24.003}}</ref>
* 20 February – Scientists report regenerating ''[[Silene stenophylla]]'' from 32,000-year-old remains. This surpasses the previous record of 2,000 years for the oldest material used to regenerate a plant.<ref>{{cite news |url=http://news.discovery.com/earth/pleistocene-plant-blooms-again-120221.html |title=It's Alive! Pleistocene Plant Blooms Again |publisher=Discovery News |date=2012-02-21 |access-date=2020-10-24 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120223160737/http://news.discovery.com/earth/pleistocene-plant-blooms-again-120221.html |archive-date=2012-02-23 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | last1=Yashina | first1=S. | last2=Gubin | first2=S. | last3=Maksimovich | first3=S. | display-authors=1| title=Regeneration of whole fertile plants from 30,000-y-old fruit tissue buried in Siberian permafrost | journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences | volume=109 | issue=10 | date=2012-02-21 | issn=0027-8424 | doi=10.1073/pnas.1118386109 | pages=4008–4013| pmid=22355102 | pmc=3309767 | bibcode=2012PNAS..109.4008Y | doi-access=free }}</ref>
* 22 February
** Scientists have extended the life of male mice by 15%, using an enzyme called SIRT6.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.newscientist.com/article/mg21328536-200-fountain-of-youth-enzyme-lengthens-mouse-life/?ignored=irrelevant|title='Fountain of youth' enzyme lengthens mouse life|publisher=New Scientist|date=2012-02-22|access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | last1=Kanfi | first1=Yariv | last2=Naiman | first2=Shoshana | last3=Amir | first3=Gail | display-authors=1| title=The sirtuin SIRT6 regulates lifespan in male mice | journal=Nature | publisher=Springer Science and Business Media LLC | volume=483 | issue=7388 | date=2012-02-22 | issn=0028-0836 | doi=10.1038/nature10815 | pages=218–221| pmid=22367546 | bibcode=2012Natur.483..218K | s2cid=4417564 }}</ref>
** Engineers at Stanford University reveal a wirelessly powered, self-propelled medical device that can travel through the bloodstream to deliver drugs, perform diagnostics or microsurgeries.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2012/02/120222094250.htm|title=Tiny, implantable medical device can propel itself through bloodstream|publisher=Science Daily|date=2012-02-22|access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|title=Tiny, implantable medical device can propel itself through bloodstream|url=http://engineering.stanford.edu/news/implantable-wirelessly-powered-self-propelled-medical-device|publisher=Stanford University|author=Andrew Myers|date=2012-02-22|access-date=2020-10-24|archive-date=21 October 2012|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121021064522/http://engineering.stanford.edu/news/implantable-wirelessly-powered-self-propelled-medical-device|url-status=dead}}</ref>
** [[NASA]] reports the detection of the solid form of [[buckminsterfullerene|buckyballs (buckminsterfullerene)]] in [[outer space|deep space]].<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.space.com/14652-space-soccer-balls-buckyballs-everest.html|title=Tiny 'Soccer Ball' Space Molecules Could Equal 10,000 Mount Everests|publisher=Space.com|date=2012-02-22|access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|url=http://www.nasa.gov/home/hqnews/2012/feb/HQ_12-057_Spitzer_Buckyballs.html|title=NASA'S Spitzer Finds Solid Buckyballs in Space|publisher=NASA|date=2012-02-22|access-date=2020-10-24|archive-date=16 April 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230416132123/https://www.nasa.gov/home/hqnews/2012/feb/HQ_12-057_Spitzer_Buckyballs.html|url-status=dead}}</ref>
** Researchers show that [[sirtuin]], a class of proteins, is directly linked to longevity in mammals.<ref>{{cite news|url=http://www.zmescience.com/medicine/genetic/sirtuin-longevity-gene-sirt6-mice-931235/|title=Sirtuin back in the highlight as longevity gene – overexpression prelongs male mice lifespan by 15%|publisher=ZME Science|author=Tibi Puiu|date=2017-10-27|access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | last=Ledford | first=Heidi | title=Sirtuin protein linked to longevity in mammals | journal=Nature | publisher=Springer Science and Business Media LLC | date=2012-02-22 | issn=0028-0836 | doi=10.1038/nature.2012.10074 | s2cid=85075638 | doi-access=free }}</ref>
* 24 February – British-Italian researchers demonstrate a giant 3D printer capable of constructing a full-sized house in a single 24-hour session. The machine, which uses [[sand]] and a chemical binder as its working material, prints structures from the ground up, including stairs, partition walls and even piping cavities.<ref>{{cite news|url=http://www.gizmag.com/d-shape-3d-printer/21594/|title=D-Shape 3D printer can print full-sized houses|publisher=New Atlas/Gizmag|author=Jan Belezina|date=2012-02-24|access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref>
* 26 February
** Researchers publish the first images of the charge distribution in a single molecule, precisely showing the motion of [[electrons]]. The observed distribution apparently corresponds closely with predictive models.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/science-environment-17156036|title=Single molecule's electric charges seen in first image|publisher=BBC News|date=2012-02-26|access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | last1=Mohn | first1=Fabian | last2=Gross | first2=Leo | last3=Moll | first3=Nikolaj | display-authors=1| title=Imaging the charge distribution within a single molecule | journal=Nature Nanotechnology | publisher=Springer Science and Business Media LLC | volume=7 | issue=4 | date=2012-02-26 | issn=1748-3387 | doi=10.1038/nnano.2012.20 | pages=227–231| pmid=22367099 | bibcode=2012NatNa...7..227M }}</ref>
** It may be possible to one day create an "unlimited" supply of [[human egg]]s to aid [[fertility]] treatment, US doctors say.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/health-17152413|title=Unlimited human eggs 'potential' for fertility treatment|publisher=BBC News|author=James Gallagher|date=2012-02-26|access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | last1=White | first1=Yvonne A R | last2=Woods | first2=Dori C | last3=Takai | first3=Yasushi | display-authors=1 | title=Oocyte formation by mitotically active germ cells purified from ovaries of reproductive-age women | journal=Nature Medicine | publisher=Springer Science and Business Media LLC | volume=18 | issue=3 | date=2012-02-26 | issn=1078-8956 | doi=10.1038/nm.2669 | pages=413–421| pmid=22366948 | pmc=3296965 }}</ref>
* 27 February
** The remains of two new species of prehistoric [[penguin]] are discovered – ''[[Kairuku grebneffi]]'' and ''[[Kairuku waitaki]]''. Standing nearly {{convert|5|ft|m}} tall, ''Kairuku grebneffi'' is the largest penguin ever discovered.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://news.discovery.com/animals/penguin-tallest-fattest-new-zealand-120227.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120301011529/http://news.discovery.com/animals/penguin-tallest-fattest-new-zealand-120227.html|archive-date=2012-03-01|title= Ancient Penguin Weighed 130 Pounds|author=Jennifer Viegas|work=Discovery News |date=2012-02-27|access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal| last1=Ksepka | first1=Daniel T. | last2=Fordyce | first2=R. Ewan | last3=Ando | first3=Tatsuro | display-authors=1| title=New fossil penguins (Aves, Sphenisciformes) from the Oligocene of New Zealand reveal the skeletal plan of stem penguins | journal=Journal of Vertebrate Paleontology | publisher=Informa UK Limited | volume=32 | issue=2 | date=2012-02-28 | issn=0272-4634 | doi=10.1080/02724634.2012.652051 | pages=235–254| bibcode=2012JVPal..32..235K | s2cid=85887012}}</ref>
* 28 February
** [[IBM]] announces a breakthrough in [[quantum computing]], demonstrating a [[qubit]] microchip that can preserve its quantum states up to four times longer than previous designs.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www-03.ibm.com/press/us/en/pressrelease/36901.wss|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120228193934/http://www-03.ibm.com/press/us/en/pressrelease/36901.wss|archive-date=28 February 2012|title=IBM Research Advances Device Performance for Quantum Computing|publisher=IBM|date=2012-02-28|access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.kurzweilai.net/ibm-research-achieves-new-record-for-quantum-computing-device-performance|title=IBM Research achieves new record for quantum computing device performance|publisher=Kurzweil AI|date=2012-02-28|access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref>
** Researchers estimate that ''[[Tyrannosaurus rex]]''{{'}}s bite force could exceed 57,000 [[newton (unit)|newton]]s, more than three times that of a [[great white shark]].<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/science/dinosaurs/9110811/T-Rexs-bite-was-three-times-greater-than-shark.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120301214156/https://www.telegraph.co.uk/science/dinosaurs/9110811/T-Rexs-bite-was-three-times-greater-than-shark.html/|archive-date=2012-03-01|title=T-Rex's bite was 'three times greater than shark'|work=The Telegraph|date=2012-02-29|access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | last1=Bates | first1=K. T. | last2=Falkingham | first2=P. L. | title=Estimating maximum bite performance in Tyrannosaurus rex using multi-body dynamics | journal=Biology Letters | publisher=The Royal Society | volume=8 | issue=4 | date=2012-02-29 | issn=1744-9561 | doi=10.1098/rsbl.2012.0056 | pages=660–664| pmid=22378742 | pmc=3391458 | bibcode=2012BiLet...8..660B }}</ref>
* 29 February – [[Raspberry Pi]] [[single-board computer]] is commercially launched through U.K. online retailers.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/technology-17190918|title=The Raspberry Pi computer goes on general sale|publisher=BBC News|date=2012-02-29|access-date=2020-10-24}}</ref>

===March===
[[File:Western Lowland Gorilla at Bronx Zoo 2 cropped.jpg|thumb|250px|7 March 2012: scientists [[sequence the genome]] of the [[western gorilla]].]]
* 1 March – New research concludes that the Earth's oceans may be [[Ocean acidification|growing more acidic]] at a faster rate than at any time in the past 300 million years.<ref>{{cite web|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120303063007/http://www.msnbc.msn.com/id/46594944/ns/us_news-environment/|publisher=MSNBC|title=Oceans' acidic shift may be fastest in 300 million years |author=Deborah Zabarenko|date=2012-03-01|archive-date=2012-03-03|url=http://www.msnbc.msn.com/id/46594944/ns/us_news-environment/}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | last1=Honisch | first1=B. | last2=Ridgwell | first2=A. | last3=Schmidt | first3=D. N. | display-authors=1| title=The Geological Record of Ocean Acidification | journal=Science | publisher=American Association for the Advancement of Science (AAAS) | volume=335 | issue=6072 | date=2012-03-01 | issn=0036-8075 | doi=10.1126/science.1208277 | pages=1058–1063| pmid=22383840 | bibcode=2012Sci...335.1058H | hdl=1874/385704 | s2cid=6361097 | hdl-access=free }}</ref>
* 2 March
** NASA's ''[[Cassini (spacecraft)|Cassini]]'' spacecraft detects [[oxygen]] in the atmosphere of [[Saturn]]'s moon [[Dione (moon)|Dione]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/science-environment-17225127|title=Oxygen envelops Saturn's icy moon|publisher=BBC|author=Pallab Ghosh|date=2012-03-06|access-date=2021-05-29}}</ref>
** [[Meta-analysis]] of 42 previous studies concludes that some consumption of [[chocolate]] may be good for the [[heart]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.reuters.com/article/us-chocolate-heart-idUSTRE8210O420120302|work=Reuters|date=2012-03-02|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120303004553/https://www.reuters.com/article/2012/03/02/us-chocolate-heart-idUSTRE8210O420120302|archive-date=2012-03-03|title=Chocolate may be good for your heart: study|author=Amy Norton}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | last1=Hooper | first1=Lee | last2=Kay | first2=Colin | last3=Abdelhamid | first3=Asmaa | display-authors=1| title=Effects of chocolate, cocoa, and flavan-3-ols on cardiovascular health: a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized trials | journal=The American Journal of Clinical Nutrition | publisher=Oxford University Press (OUP) | volume=95 | issue=3 | date=2012-02-01 | issn=0002-9165 | doi=10.3945/ajcn.111.023457 | pages=740–751| pmid=22301923 |doi-access=free}}</ref>
* 5 March – A study finds a [[correlation]] between [[snoring]] as a [[toddler]] and behavior problems later in childhood.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/health-17237576|title='Child behaviour link' to snoring|publisher=BBC|date=2012-03-05|access-date=2021-05-29}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | last1=Bonuck | first1=Karen | last2=Freeman | first2=Katherine | last3=Chervin | first3=Ronald D. | display-authors=1| title=Sleep-Disordered Breathing in a Population-Based Cohort: Behavioral Outcomes at 4 and 7 Years | journal=Pediatrics | publisher=American Academy of Pediatrics (AAP) | volume=129 | issue=4 | date=2012-03-05 | issn=0031-4005 | doi=10.1542/peds.2011-1402 | pages=e857–e865| pmid=22392181 | pmc=3313633 }}</ref>
* 7 March
** Physicists from [[Fermi National Accelerator Laboratory]] report data suggesting that the elusive hypothesized [[Higgs boson]] ("[[Higgs boson#"The God particle"|God particle]]", with a mass of 115 to {{val|135|u=GeV/c2}}) may have been detected.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2012/03/07/science/higgs-boson-may-be-indicated-in-new-data.html|title=Data Hint at Hypothetical Particle, Key to Mass in the Universe|work=The New York Times|author=Dennis Overbye|date=2012-03-07|access-date=2021-05-29}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.interactions.org/cms/?pid=1031511|publisher=Interactions.org|title=Tevatron experiments report latest results in search for Higgs boson |date=2012-03-06|access-date=2021-05-29}}</ref>
